rows.Name: Osteomyelitis
rows.Description: Severe infection of the bone, marrow, and surrounding tissue
rows.Etiology: Most commonly staphylococcus aureus through direct or indirect infection (indirect more common in peds)
Risk Factors: Open wounds, Recent surgery, Pressure injuries
Pathophysiology: 4. dead bone separates and becomes sequestra (island surrounded by purulent drainage)
Treatment: IV antibiotics based on cultures, 4 – 6 weeks (sometimes up to 3 to 6 months)

rows.Name: Osteoporosis
Etiology: 1st degree / primary - occurs in women after menopause and men later in life, result of failure to develop optimal peak bone mass earlier in life
2nd degree / secondary - result of medications or other conditions that affect bone metabolism (eg. hyperparathyroidism)
Risk Factors: Medical conditions that reduce calcium absorption
Pathophysiology: Osteoblasts (bone creation) cant keep up with osteoclasts (bone destruction)
Diagnostics: DEXA (Dual Energy Xray Absorptiometry) bone density scan
Quantitative ultrasound (measures density in heel, kneecap, or shin)
Clinical Manifestations: Loss of height (spinal compression and kyphosis)
Treatment: Hormone replacement therapy - must be weighted against risks
Calcitonin IM or intranasal - inhibits osteoclasts (give with calcium supplement to prevent secondary hyperparathyroidism)
Bisphosphonates - binds to bones and poisons osteoclasts (take with water, remain upright for 30 min)

rows.Name: Osteoarthritis
rows.Description: “wear and tear”. Cartilage wears down over time until bone on bone contact
Pathophysiology: Cartilage goes from smooth, white, translucent -> dull, yellow, granular
Diagnostics: CBC, renal, LFT to rule out other causes
Management: Weight reduction to reduce joint strain
Mild pain - acetaminophen, NSAID (celecoxib), topical analgesic
Moderate pain - intra-articular corticosteroid injections, hyaluronate injection, supportive devices, tramadol, duloxetine, colchicine
Intractable pain / loss of function - opioids, surgery

rows.Name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
rows.Description: Chronic systemic autoimmune disorder, inflammation of connective tissue in synovial joints.
Etiology: Unknown, likely combo of environmental and genetic factors
Flare ups can affect other areas
Pathophysiology: 1. Antigen triggers the formation of abnormal IgG
2. Antibodies form against IgG known as rheumatoid factor
3. IgG and RF join to form inflammation in joints
4. Neutrophils go to inflamed area, inflammation causes synovial lining to thicken
Diagnostics: CRP, ESR, ANA titres, anti-citrullinated protein antibody
Clinical Manifestations: Leads to deformity and disability: Ulnar drift (fingers towards pinky), Boutonniere deformity (middle finger joint stays flexed), Hallux valgus (bunion), Swan neck (middle finger joint hyper extended, outer joint flexed)
Sjogren Syndrome - severe dry eyes and mouth (immune system destroys moisture glands)
Felty Syndrome - splenomegaly and low WBC
Treatment: DMARDS (methotrexate [marrow suppression, hepatotoxicity], sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine, leflunomide)
TNF drugs (also a DMARD, block TNF-α protein to reduce inflammation) - adalimumab, etanercept
Management: Hospitalization for systemic complications if necessary

rows.Name: Soft Tissue Injury
Management: Limit movement, but prolonged rest is not necessary
rows.Notes: Sprains may result in an evulsion fracture where ligament pulls away piece of bone

rows.Name: Fractures
rows.Neurovascular Assessment: Neurovascular assessment - extremities (color, temp, pulse, edema, sensation, strength)
Clinical Manifestations: Inability to bear weight or use affected part
May not have obvious bone deformity
Healing Process: 3. Callus forms (contains: calcium, phosphorus, magnesium) - ossification begins
4. Ossification occurs 3 weeks to 6 months post fracture, visible on Xray, mobility still limited
6. Remodelling - can take up to year for the bone to heal completely

rows.Name: Traction
rows.Description: Application of a pulling force to an injured body part, while counter traction pulls in opposite direction
Traction notes (skin): Used for short term treatment or until skeletal traction surgery is possible
Tape, boots, splints used to maintain alignment
Traction notes (skeletal): Can be used to treat contractures or hip dysplasia
Pin or wire inserted into bone
Too much weight can impair healing (delay or prevent)
Nursing: 2. Ropes hang freely, weights off the ground (R)
7. Overhead grips for pt to reposition (O)

rows.Name: Cast Care
Cast Disadvantages (plaster): Takes 24 - 48 hrs to dry (large may take up to 72 hrs)
Weight bearing must be delayed until dry
Prone to cracking or crumbling (especially at edges)
Cast Disadvantages (fiberglass): Not recommended for severe injuries or if excessive swelling
Macerates skin if padding becomes wet
Edges can be sharp and cause abrasion
Assessment: 6 Ps Neurovascular assessment (compartment syndrome, surgical fasciotomy)
Management: Moisture - keep dry and avoid moisture inside (mold / infection risk)
Leave uncovered until dry (delays drying, can cause overheating or burns)
Elevate on pillow to reduce swelling
If it gets wet, use blow dryer on COOL setting
Do not insert anything into the cast
Don’t write or draw on fiberglass
Circle areas of drainage to assess progression
Encourage ROM and movement within limits of injury

rows.Name: Compartment Syndrome
rows.Description: Circulation cut off by edema in confined space, like a cast
rows.Etiology: Blood and swelling fills muscles that compress vessels and prevent circulation
Clinical Manifestations: Severe pain (worse with use or stretching)

rows.Name: Hip Fractures
rows.Description: Usually a fracture of the acetabulum (socket)
Clinical Manifestations: Inability to get up from fall, walk, or weight bear
Severe pain in hip or groin
Injured side leg is shorter, or abducted
Management: Healing takes 6 - 12 weeks, no baths or driving for 4 - 6 weeks
Risk for dislocation after (avoid crossing legs, bending over for shoes, sitting on low seats or toilets, sitting or standing bent more than 90 deg)
Notes: One of longest average hospital stays
One in four will die from complications within one year
Half of survivors will require long term care
